checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully extracts the text from the source PDF. All headings, paragraphs, and list items in the output can be traced back to the corresponding sections on pages 1, 2, and 3 of the source document. There are no invented section headings or body text beyond the allowed AI-generated metadata (titles and alt text).
The source document contains no phone numbers, email addresses, or URLs. The output JSON also contains none of these elements, thus there is no fabrication.

The output JSON contains three sections corresponding to the three pages of the source PDF. Section 1 covers the '2024 Data Collection' (Page 1), Section 2 covers the '2025 Data Collection Area' map (Page 2), and Section 3 covers the 'North Carolina Collaboratory' details (Page 3). All content from the source is represented.
There are no tables in the source document. This check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: A careful comparison shows no significant text omissions. The bulleted list on page 3 of the source contains 8 items, all of which are present in the 'list' element of the third section in the JSON. The text on page 1 regarding datums and quality levels is also fully present.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON is a very accurate representation of the source. For instance, the superscript 'th' in '4th phase' on Page 1 is correctly handled using the 'superscript' property in the JSON. There are no wording changes that alter the meaning of the document.

checks.a_analysis: The output follows the source document structure across three pages. Each page's main title is used as a section title. In Section 3, the title is a concatenation of the two header lines found on Page 3 of the PDF ('2025 Data Collection Area' and 'North Carolina Collaboratory'), and these are repeated as Heading level 2 elements within the section. This is a structural choice of the pipeline to facilitate navigation and is not considered a defect of content duplication, as the text elements represent the visual layout of the source. No unintentional duplication of paragraphs or list items was found.

The heading hierarchy is logical and consistent with the source document. Section titles represent the top-level organization. In Section 3, sub-headings (H2) are used for the dual titles found on the page, correctly nesting them under the section's synthesized title. There are no skipped levels (e.g., H1 to H3).
The source document does not contain any tables. The output correctly reflects this absence.

The output preserves the reading order of the source PDF. On Page 1, it follows the title, the map, and then the descriptive text boxes. On Page 2, it follows the title and the map. On Page 3, it follows the titles, the map, and the bulleted list. The flow of information is logical and mirrors the original intent.

The bulleted list on Page 3 of the source document, containing 8 distinct points, has been correctly converted into a single list object with 8 items. The text within each list item matches the source, and there is no incorrect merging or splitting of points.
The source document does not contain any functional links or URLs. The output correctly reflects this and has not injected any fabricated links.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization is significantly disrupted by the absence and misrendering of the primary informational graphics. In the source PDF, the maps are the central elements around which text and other logos are organized. In the HTML, the map from page 1 is missing entirely, and the maps on pages 2 and 3 are replaced by large solid green blocks. This fundamentally changes the visual organization of information and the user's ability to find and relate data spatially as intended by the source document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF features large, detailed maps as the primary content on every page (e.g., 'North Carolina LiDAR Quality Level By Phase' map on page 1).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output omits the page 1 map entirely and replaces the maps on pages 2 and 3 with solid neon green rectangles.
checks.b_evidence_location: Throughout the document, specifically corresponding to pages 1, 2, and 3 of the source.

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables; the information is presented via graphics, maps, and bulleted lists. Therefore, this check passes.

Several critical, meaningful images are either missing or broken in the HTML output. The main map from page 1 of the PDF is absent. The maps on pages 2 and 3, which show high-resolution topographic refresh areas, are rendered as solid neon green placeholders, indicating a failure to correctly process or display these images. Additionally, the 'DPS' logo from the top-left header of the source is missing from the top of the HTML output.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF includes several meaningful maps: 'North Carolina LiDAR Quality Level By Phase' (page 1) and 'High Resolution Topographic Refresh Western North Carolina' (pages 2 and 3).
checks.b_evidence_output: The map from page 1 is missing. The maps for pages 2 and 3 appear as solid neon green squares in the HTML rendering.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main body areas corresponding to pages 1, 2, and 3 of the source PDF.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content duplication (paragraphs or bulleted lists) appearing twice. While headings like '2025 Data Collection Area' appear multiple times, this is consistent with the slide titles and appropriate heading structure for accessibility in an HTML document.
